title: NOC 94105 – Metalworking and forging machine operators | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s precision manufacturing and structural steel industries are experiencing a resurgence driven by major shipbuilding contracts and the expansion of the green energy infrastructure. Metalworking and Forging Machine Operators (NOC 94105) are the technical experts who shape the heavy-duty components powering these sectors. As facilities transition to advanced CNC-integrated forging and high-precision bending, international workers with experience in automated metal forming are finding robust opportunities across the country.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

duties: Machine Operation: Setting up and operating heavy or light metalworking machines (shears, saws, plate rolls, drills, punch presses) to cut, bend, or form metal.
Forging: Operating gas or oil-fired furnaces to heat metal and using forging presses to shape metal through flattening, twisting, or piercing.
Precision Checking: Verifying products for correct shapes and dimensions against strict engineering blueprints.
Troubleshooting: Performing minor repairs and adjusting dies on anvils using overhead cranes or hoisting devices.
2026 Update: Managing automated conveyor loads and using digital interfaces to monitor forging temperatures and cycle times.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer or helper in the same company is often preferred.
Training: Several weeks of on-the-job training are standard for machine operators.
Regulation: This occupation is not regulated in Canada, meaning no mandatory provincial license is required to start working.
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS certification and site-specific heavy machinery safety training.
job_outlook: Employment is expected to remain stable with a "Moderate" growth outlook. The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) highlights that retirements will create the vast majority of vacancies, particularly in Ontario and BC where the industrial workforce is aging rapidly.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 labor market reflects a steady need for operators in heavy industrial zones:
Ontario (Toronto & Hamilton): Moderate demand. High concentration in motor vehicle parts and fabricated metal manufacturing.
British Columbia (Mainland/Southwest): Good prospects. Demand is fueled by the shipbuilding industry and structural steel fabrication for transit projects.
Alberta: Stable demand. Ongoing needs in machinery manufacturing and the fabricated metal products sector.
Quebec: Strong demand in specialized aerospace forging and primary metal manufacturing hubs.
Why Work in Canada as a Metalworking/Forging Operator?
Industrial Modernization: Access to 2026-grade technology, including high-speed hydraulic presses and AI-monitored CNC brake presses.
Diverse Employment Sectors: Opportunities range from shipbuilding and boiler manufacturing to high-end automotive parts.
Comprehensive Benefits: Approximately 91% of workers in this NOC nationwide receive medical, dental, and pension benefits.
Career Progression: Clear pathways to specialized roles like Blacksmith, Die Setter, or Production Supervisor (NOC 92023).
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (Launching April 2026): A dedicated stream for 33,000 temporary residents. Skilled trades and manufacturing workers are priority targets for this fast-track transition.
Ontario In-Demand Skills Stream: NOC 94105 is a key eligible occupation for candidates with a job offer in the province.

Express Entry (Trade Category): 2026 category-based draws continue to target individuals with experience in "Trade and Transport," providing a pathway even for TEER 4 roles through specific pilot programs.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Many heavy metalworking roles are based in regional industrial centers where community-backed PR is a high-success option.
